title: Pfizer — Vaccines — briefing Q4-2025
html:
What Changed in Pfizer Vaccines — Q4 2025

Generated: 2026-04-24 (retroactive compilation) Quarter covered: October – December 2025 Data sources: Pfizer FY 2025 earnings (Feb 3, 2026), ZoomRx Pneumococcal HCP-Pt Listening Session (Dec 2025), ZoomRx Full Report drafts (Dec 2025 - Jan 2026), industry reports Reading time: ~12 minutes

1. TL;DR (5 Bullets)

  1. Abrysvo Q4 exploded: 136% operational growth (+$270M) — driven by international launch uptake for both adult and maternal indications and favorable US adult net price. This was Abrysvo's strongest quarter ever and validated the international expansion thesis.
  2. Prevnar family grew 8% operationally in Q4 — CDC 50+ recommendation driving adult uptake in both US and international. However, "lower market share in the US" was explicitly noted by Pfizer — first earnings-call acknowledgment of Capvaxive competitive impact.
  3. ZoomRx Pneumococcal HCP-Pt data revealed critical findings — Listening session (Dec 8) and full report (Dec 24-Jan 8 versions) confirmed: assertive communication drives near-100% acceptance; Capvaxive gaining ground via serotype counting; "one and done" is the killer message; pharmacy referral is a drop-off risk.
  4. Pfizer reached 3 billionth Prevnar dose — a milestone underscoring the franchise's legacy, but also raising the question: is brand heritage enough to defend against Capvaxive's "next-generation" positioning?
  5. Full-year 2025 Pfizer revenue: $62.6B — non-COVID revenue grew 6% operationally. 2026 guidance reaffirmed at $59.5-62.5B, reflecting COVID revenue decline but continued vaccine portfolio strength.

2. Data Events That Matter

Abrysvo: Best Quarter Yet

  • What happened: Q4 2025 Abrysvo revenue grew 136% operationally, adding ~$270M vs. prior year. Growth was driven primarily by international launch uptake for both the adult and maternal indications, plus favorable net price and market share for the US adult indication.
  • What it means: Abrysvo's growth story is now firmly international. The US adult market remains constrained by the narrowed CDC recommendation, but international markets — where the recommendation landscape is more favorable — are scaling rapidly. The maternal indication is contributing meaningful growth in markets where nirsevimab access is limited.
  • What Vinoth should say: "Abrysvo's 136% Q4 growth masks a structural divergence: US is share defense, international is growth offense. Our research focus should follow the money — if international is driving growth, Pfizer may need international HCP-Pt conversation studies next."

Prevnar Family: Growing but Losing US Share

  • What happened: Prevnar family grew 8% operationally in Q4, driven by adult indication uptake (especially the 50+ expansion) in both US and international markets. But Pfizer explicitly noted "lower market share in the U.S." — the first time competitive share loss has been acknowledged on an earnings call.
  • What it means: Capvaxive is taking share. The 8% overall growth is being driven by market expansion (50+ recommendation bringing new patients in), not by competitive wins. Pfizer is growing the franchise on a rising tide while conceding share to Merck within the existing patient pool.
  • What Vinoth should say: "The 8% growth is good news, but the US share loss is the real story. Growth from market expansion can mask competitive erosion — we need to disaggregate the 50+ new-to-market patients from the switching/competitive dynamics."

ZoomRx Pneumococcal Listening Session & Full Report

  • What happened: ZoomRx conducted the mid-fielding listening session on December 8, 2025, presenting 4 selected conversations to the Pfizer team. Full report drafts were produced through late December and early January 2026, with the final v2 delivered January 16, 2026.
  • Key findings delivered:
    • Assertive vs. suggestive framing: 100% acceptance with presumptive language vs. ~60% with participatory style
    • Prevnar 20 brand dominance: Recommended by name in 12/20 conversations; "Prevnar" remains the default
    • Capvaxive competitive dynamic: When both discussed, Capvaxive wins on serotype counting (84% vs. 52%); accepted in 3/7 head-to-head conversations
    • Disease severity under-communicated: Most HCPs position pneumonia as manageable risk, not life-threatening IPD
    • "One and done" effectiveness: Most powerful single acceptance driver across all conversations
    • Pharmacy drop-off risk: 4/20 patients referred to pharmacy instead of receiving in-office; recognized as a completion barrier
    • 5-year misconception: Some HCPs incorrectly tell patients to repeat every 5 years, contradicting the "once in a lifetime" value proposition

Pfizer Full-Year 2025 Results & 2026 Guidance

  • What happened: Pfizer reported FY 2025 revenue of $62.6B (down from $63.6B, driven by COVID product decline). Non-COVID revenue grew 6% operationally. 2026 guidance: $59.5-62.5B, with COVID products expected to decline by ~$1.5B.
  • What it means: Pfizer's portfolio diversification is working — vaccine portfolio (Prevnar + Abrysvo + Penbraya) is a cornerstone of non-COVID growth. But the flat-to-declining top line creates internal pressure to maximize revenue from each franchise, making competitive share defense in pneumococcal even more critical.

3. Commercial Pulse

Metric Prevnar Family Abrysvo Capvaxive (Merck) Arexvy (GSK)
Q4 2025 Revenue +8% operationally +136% operationally (+$270M) Continued commercial ramp US sales ~40% decline in FY 2025
Full Year 2025 ~$6B+ franchise ~$1B Strong start year ~$785M (down from prior year)
Key Dynamic US share loss noted International explosion Adult-designed positioning Narrowed CDC impact

Client Financial Signals — Pfizer Q4 2025 / FY 2025 Earnings Call

What Pfizer management said on the Q4 2025 / FY 2025 call:

  1. "Vaccines remain a core growth engine" — management framed the full vaccine portfolio (Prevnar + Abrysvo + Penbraya) as the primary non-COVID growth pillar. Full-year 2025 revenue was ~$63B, with Prevnar at ~$6.2B and Abrysvo at ~$750M (mostly older adults).
  2. Abrysvo maternal was effectively conceded — management stated "the monoclonal approach (Beyfortus/Enflonsia) has become preferred for infant RSV," the most explicit acknowledgment yet that the maternal vaccination strategy has lost the infant RSV battle. The RSV franchise is now being repositioned entirely around older adults.
  3. Prevnar 20 maintained 80%+ pneumococcal share vs Vaxneuvance — management cited continued dominance over Merck's PCV15, but notably did not address Capvaxive (PCV21) share erosion with the same confidence. The "lower US market share" comment (first-ever earnings call acknowledgment) was buried in prepared remarks, not highlighted.
  4. Penbraya guided toward $500M+ peak sales — management positioned Penbraya's pentavalent convenience as the key to unlocking the MenB market, projecting the franchise on a path to meaningful revenue contribution within 2-3 years.

Key analyst Q&A signals:

  1. "Is Abrysvo maternal dead?" — the defining analyst question of the call. Pfizer's response was carefully hedged: "We're evaluating the data and the competitive landscape." The non-denial effectively confirmed that maternal RSV is no longer a growth priority. Analysts interpreted this as a soft admission.
  2. Capvaxive share impact was questioned more aggressively than Q3 — analysts pressed on whether the 80%+ share figure included or excluded the Capvaxive impact. Management's response focused on market expansion (50+ recommendation) rather than head-to-head competitive dynamics, a classic deflection when the news is unfavorable.
  3. 2026 guidance range ($59.5-62.5B) raised concerns about vaccine franchise trajectory — the flat-to-declining top line creates internal pressure to maximize every franchise. Analysts noted the paradox: vaccine growth is real, but it's insufficient to offset COVID revenue decline.

4. Competitor Earnings Intelligence

Revenue Snapshot — Q4 2025

Company Brand Q4 2025 Revenue YoY Change Key Signal
GSK Arexvy (RSV) ~$600M Peak season dominant GSK winning RSV older adult market
GSK Bexsero (MenB) ~$300M Stable Holding against Penbraya in MenB
GSK Shingrix ~$1.1B Consistent GSK vaccine franchise at seasonal peak
Merck Vaxneuvance (PCV15) ~$180M Modest growth Prevnar 20 maintains 80%+ pneumococcal share
Merck Gardasil ~$1.5B China recovery slow Merck vaccine portfolio under pressure
Merck Enflonsia (clesrovimab) Newly approved — Emerging competitor to Abrysvo maternal
Moderna mResvia (mRNA RSV) ~$200M First full season Modest uptake vs Arexvy/Abrysvo
Sanofi/AZ Beyfortus (nirsevimab) ~$400M Peak season Establishing standard of care for infant RSV
Abrysvo +136% operational Best quarter ever International-led growth explosion
Prevnar Family +8% operational Growing but losing US share First earnings acknowledgment of Capvaxive impact

GSK — Arexvy ($600M) + Bexsero ($300M) + Shingrix (~$1.1B)

GSK Q4 2025 earnings:

  1. Arexvy at ~$600M — dominant during peak RSV season: Q4 is peak RSV vaccination season, and Arexvy's first-mover advantage is fully visible. GSK is winning the older adult RSV market with established physician familiarity, formulary positioning, and the seasonal vaccination workflow already built around Arexvy. Pfizer's Abrysvo grew 136% in Q4 but primarily through international markets — in the US, Arexvy remains the default RSV choice for many providers.

  2. Bexsero at ~$300M, holding against Penbraya: Despite Penbraya's pentavalent convenience positioning, Bexsero's established franchise and proven safety record continue to hold share in MenB. The competitive dynamic hasn't shifted materially.

  3. GSK vaccine franchise at seasonal peak (~$2B+ in Q4): The combined Arexvy + Bexsero + Shingrix franchise demonstrates GSK's unmatched seasonal vaccine portfolio depth. Shingrix at $1.1B remains the anchor.

Implication for Pfizer: Abrysvo's 136% growth masks a structural divergence: international markets are driving growth while the US older adult RSV market remains GSK-dominated. Pfizer's US RSV strategy needs a reset if Arexvy continues to capture the "default" position during vaccination season.

Merck — Vaxneuvance ($180M) + Gardasil ($1.5B) + Enflonsia (newly approved)

Merck Q4 2025 earnings:

  1. Vaxneuvance at ~$180M — still struggling: Prevnar 20 continues to maintain 80%+ pneumococcal market share. Vaxneuvance (PCV15) has not found a meaningful differentiation pathway against the broader-coverage Prevnar 20.

  2. Gardasil at ~$1.5B — China recovery slow: Gardasil's headwinds from China continue. This is relevant to Pfizer only insofar as it increases Merck's incentive to invest aggressively in Capvaxive as a growth engine within their vaccine portfolio.

  3. Enflonsia approved for infant RSV — a new competitive dimension: Merck's clesrovimab (monoclonal antibody for infant RSV) received approval, adding another monoclonal approach to the infant RSV protection landscape alongside Beyfortus. This further undermines the maternal vaccination approach (Abrysvo) — now two monoclonal options compete for the "infant RSV" market segment that Abrysvo's maternal indication targets.

Implication for Pfizer: Enflonsia's approval is a significant negative signal for Abrysvo maternal. The infant RSV market now has two monoclonal antibody options (Beyfortus + Enflonsia) competing against maternal vaccination. The monoclonal approach is winning the preference battle with pediatricians — direct infant immunization is simpler and more predictable.

Moderna — mResvia (~$200M)

Moderna Q4 2025 earnings:

  1. mResvia at ~$200M in first full RSV season: Moderna achieved modest but real commercial uptake during the 2025-26 RSV season. However, $200M vs Arexvy's ~$600M and Abrysvo's international explosion shows mResvia captured a distant third-place position.

  2. Moderna positioning mRNA platform advantage for the long game: Management emphasized annual revaccination potential and the RSV+flu combination pipeline (mRNA-1345). The pitch is that mRNA technology enables iterative improvement and multi-pathogen combination — advantages that protein-based vaccines (Arexvy, Abrysvo) can't easily replicate.

  3. Modest uptake acknowledged but long-term confidence maintained: Moderna's management was transparent about slower-than-expected RSV uptake but framed it as "year one" of a platform build, not a product failure.

Implication for Pfizer: mResvia's $200M season confirms it's a third-place competitor, not a market disruptor — this season. But Moderna's combination pipeline (RSV+flu) is the structural threat. If a single-shot RSV+flu vaccine launches, the standalone RSV market contracts, and Abrysvo faces category-level disruption.

Sanofi/AstraZeneca — Beyfortus (~$400M)

Sanofi Q4 2025 earnings:

  1. Beyfortus at ~$400M during peak season — establishing standard of care: With adequate supply for the 2025-26 season, Beyfortus achieved the commercial validation that the supply-constrained first season couldn't deliver. Pediatricians are adopting Beyfortus as the default infant RSV protection approach.

  2. Monoclonal approach winning against maternal vaccination: The competitive dynamic is becoming clear — pediatricians prefer directly immunizing infants over the maternal vaccination pathway. The reasons are practical: no dependency on timing of maternal dose, predictable infant coverage, simpler logistics.

  3. Abrysvo maternal indication under significant pressure: With both Beyfortus ($400M peak) and now Enflonsia entering the market, Pfizer's maternal vaccination approach for infant RSV faces a two-front monoclonal challenge.

Implication for Pfizer: Q4 2025 may represent the quarter where Abrysvo's maternal RSV strategy was definitively outcompeted by the monoclonal approach. Pfizer's infant RSV revenue depends on international markets where Beyfortus/Enflonsia access is limited — a shrinking advantage as both products expand globally.

Synthesis: What the Combined Picture Tells Us

  1. RSV competitive dynamics have crystallized in Q4 2025 — Arexvy dominates US older adult RSV, Beyfortus is establishing standard of care for infant protection, and Abrysvo's growth story is entirely international. Pfizer's US RSV position is structurally weaker than competitors in both segments.

  2. The infant RSV market has shifted from Abrysvo maternal vs Beyfortus to a two-monoclonal standard — Enflonsia's approval gives pediatricians a second monoclonal option, further marginalizing maternal vaccination. This thesis (Abrysvo maternal won't recover in US) is now strongly supported.

  3. Pneumococcal share erosion is now earnings-confirmed — Pfizer's acknowledgment of "lower US market share" for Prevnar combined with Merck's vaccine portfolio pressure (Gardasil weak) creates a dynamic where Merck will invest disproportionately in Capvaxive, the one franchise with growth potential.

  4. Moderna's combination pipeline is the wild card — mResvia at $200M is a third-place player today, but RSV+flu combination potential could restructure the entire market in 2-3 years. Pfizer has no equivalent combination strategy.

5. Thesis Update

Thesis Status Key Evidence This Quarter
1. Capvaxive serotype counting erodes Prevnar 20 STRENGTHENED Pfizer acknowledges US share loss; ZoomRx data shows serotype counting dynamic in action; Capvaxive wins 3/7 head-to-head conversations
2. Abrysvo maternal won't recover UNCHANGED (US) No US maternal-specific data; international maternal growth in Q4
3. Adult RSV market structurally capped SUPPORTED Arexvy US sales declined ~40%; mResvia essentially failed ($8M FY); only Abrysvo growing via international + label expansion
4. Presumptive communication = highest ROI STRONGLY SUPPORTED ZoomRx data confirms: assertive = 100% acceptance, suggestive = ~60%. Pattern confirmed across 20 pneumococcal conversations
5. Penbraya constrained by MenB apathy UNCHANGED No new primary data

Net assessment: Q4 2025 is the quarter where the Capvaxive competitive thesis moved from "emerging" to "confirmed in conversation data." Pfizer's Prevnar franchise is growing but ceding share — the market expansion from 50+ is masking competitive erosion. The ZoomRx data from this quarter provides the most granular evidence available on how the competitive dynamic is playing out at the physician-patient level.

6. What to Watch Next Quarter (Q1 2026)

  1. ZoomRx Pneumococcal Final Report v2 delivery and client discussion — delivered January 16, 2026. Watch for Pfizer's reaction to the Capvaxive competitive findings and the communication style recommendation.
  2. Pfizer Q1 2026 earnings (late April 2026) — first quarterly read on 2026 vaccine trajectory. Watch for Prevnar share commentary and Abrysvo seasonal wind-down.
  3. Capvaxive quarterly sales — Merck Q4 2025 / Q1 2026 results will quantify the competitive ramp. If Capvaxive exceeds $200M quarterly, the share shift is accelerating.
  4. Vaxcyte VAX-31 Phase 3 updates — enrollment progress and any interim signals. FDA Breakthrough Therapy designation adds urgency to Pfizer's competitive positioning.
  5. ACIP deliberations — any revisiting of RSV adult recommendation or pneumococcal 50+ implementation will have direct market impact.

Back to TA Knowledge Hub | Home

Net assessment: Q4 2025 is the quarter where the Capvaxive competitive thesis moved from "emerging" to "confirmed in conversation data." Pfizer's Prevnar franchise is growing but ceding share — the market expansion from 50+ is masking competitive erosion. The ZoomRx data from this quarter provides the most granular evidence available on how the competitive dynamic is playing out at the physician-patient level.
